hook: A 72-hour response window isn't a marketing line — it's a procurement requirement.
Most CO calls aren't answered inside 72 hours. We treat that as the bar, not the goal.
On-call vendors that don't answer the call aren't vendors. They're paperwork.
Your capture deadline is Friday. Your sub still hasn't sent reps and certs. We send ours inside 24 hours.
When a pest sighting hits your front desk at 8 AM, the question is who picks up the phone.
Trucks staged. Crew briefed. Paperwork ready. That's a response posture.
SDVOSB certified through SBA VetCert. That's the only veteran-owned status that counts at the procurement desk.
The federal SDVOSB goal is 3%. Most agencies are still missing it. We're built for the actions that close that gap.
MBE. SBE. DBE. SDVOSB. HUBZone (where applicable). One vendor identity. Five doors into your set-aside utilization.
Your subcontracting plan goals don't fill themselves. We close the SDVOSB and MBE columns without pass-through risk.
Set-aside utilization isn't a number on a quarterly report. It's whether the right vendor is in your pool today.
Facility readiness is a posture, not a project. Here's the checklist we run on every onboarding.
Three things every facility needs before next quarter: pest plan, janitorial overflow, emergency standby.
Vendor backup posture is the cheapest insurance you'll buy this year.
ADA-aware. Eco-friendly. Occupant-safe. That's what a public facility service crew should look like.
Your primary vendor is great — until they're not available. Backup posture is what protects occupancy.
Integrated Pest Management 101: prevention first, treatment second, occupants safe at every step.
Eco-friendly treatment in occupied space isn't a slogan. It's a workflow.
NAICS 561710 is our primary code. Pest control isn't a side lane for us — it's the lane.
Quarterly pest service is cheaper than three reactive call-outs. Here's how we structure it.
IPM in a public building looks like this: inspection, evidence, plan, treatment, follow-up.
A clean teaming package at proposal time looks like this: cert proof, insurance posture, defined scope, single POC.
If your SDVOSB sub is a pass-through, your subcontracting plan is theater. We self-perform a defined scope.
Your SBLO wants three things: current certs, signed teaming agreement, performance during execution. We bring all three.
Capture week shouldn't be the first time you talk to your sub. Open the teaming discussion now.
Micro-purchase vendor readiness is a five-line checklist. Most vendors miss line two.
Your purchase card is the most flexible tool in your kit. Make sure your small business bench can keep up.
At or below the general FAR micro-purchase threshold (currently $15,000; exceptions apply): documented, certified, responsive. Those filters move first.
Year-end is six weeks from now. Build the micro-purchase bench in week one, not week five.

SDVOSB sole-source under simplified acquisition procedures — a clean pathway when scope and the current Simplified Acquisition Threshold align (verify the current SAT at FAR 2.101 before quoting any dollar value).
An RFQ response without a current capability statement is a wasted Tuesday. Ours is one click away.

Under-$250K work is its own discipline — operator preference, not a legal claim. Different vendor profile, different documentation, different speed. Verify the current Simplified Acquisition Threshold at FAR 2.101 before any external dollar-value statement.
Your SAT bench needs SDVOSB depth. We're in the SAM.gov record, certified, and ready to respond.
Standby posture isn't a 24/7 promise. It's a documented mobilization window with a real POC.
Storm season is the wrong week to find an emergency vendor. Add ARCG to the standby roster now.
Post-event facility standup needs three lanes at once: janitorial, staffing, translation. One vendor can hold the line.
Crew. Trucks. PPE. Documentation. That's what a standby posture actually looks like.
Displacement events need a translator on day one. We bring that lane to the table.
A capability statement is a single page with seven blocks. We'll show you the version we send to COs.
Reps and certs hygiene: check SAM.gov quarterly, not annually. That alone keeps half your bench alive.
If your sub can't send insurance, W-9, and reps in 24 hours, your capture is already behind.
NAICS alignment is the cheapest win in procurement. We pair our codes to your requirement, not the other way around.
Vendor documentation isn't a marketing exercise. It's the difference between a 30-minute award and a 30-day audit.
Veteran-owned shows up in the reps and certs differently. SDVOSB through SBA VetCert is what your filter is looking for.
Operational discipline is the unsexy version of veteran-owned: promises stated, kept, documented.
We're not pitching veteran-owned as a brand story. We're operating it as a documentation standard.
When we say we'll show up at 7 AM, we show up at 6:45 with the paperwork already signed.
Veteran-owned doesn't have to be loud. It's just visible in the way the work gets done.
24-hour cert package. 72-hour mobilization window. Stated in writing on the capability statement.
Five certifications, one vendor identity. Less paperwork on your side. More set-aside math on ours.
An on-call vendor pool is only useful if the vendors actually answer. That's where most pools fall apart.
Pest sightings are reputational events, not just facility events. Treat them that way.
Your next IDIQ has a small business floor. We help you hit it without scrambling at proposal time.
Two-line micro-purchase rule: vendor is in SAM.gov, vendor answers the phone. Everything else is bonus.
SAT actions don't need multi-volume RFPs. They need a vendor who can read the SOW and quote on Tuesday.
Standby is a posture. Posture takes preparation. Preparation looks like this.
Vendor onboarding goes faster when the documentation lands clean. Here's what we send on day one.
Discipline in this business looks small. It's a callback inside the hour. A capability statement that matches the NAICS. A cert that doesn't expire.
Three days is the longest a procurement lead should wait for a callback. We treat it as the ceiling, not the goal.
Set-aside compliance shouldn't be a fire drill in week eight. Open the teaming conversation in week one.
Five lanes, one vendor identity. That's a simpler call sheet on the day you need it.
Pest control isn't a one-time visit. It's a documented service plan. Here's how we structure the cadence.
The right sub at the right time saves your subcontracting plan. The wrong one costs you the audit.
Micro-purchase is where small business goals actually get hit. Build the bench like it matters.
Pre-positioned vendor agreements are quiet wins. Until the event happens. Then they're the only thing that matters.
